summary: Independently rebuilt context from gh issue #49, the diff (0ed8461 over 2e415e5), current full engine/graph/edgelog.go and compact.go, and the prior 4.5.11.1 verification (036-verification, confirming a genuinely separate bug already fixed). Traced the actual lock acquisition/release sequence by direct source read (not implementer claims): Compact acquires log.LockNode(id) immediately before ReadNodeAfter and, for every node with maxSeg>=0, keeps that same unlock func in heldNodeLocks - never releasing and reacquiring - all the way through that node's own TruncateNode call in the final loop; released immediately only for maxSeg<0 nodes (nothing to protect). AppendEdge acquires the identical per-node lock (same nodeLocks map, same sourceFileID key via nodeLock) for its entire body, including the wal.Writer.Append call which engine/wal/writer.go documents as fsyncing before return - not a partial hold. Ran the new test at -race -count=30 (30/30 pass), the full engine/graph suite at -race -count=2 (pass), and the full engine module at -race (pass, all packages). go build/go vet/gofmt all clean. Wrote and ran three throwaway (uncommitted, removed after use) adversarial probes beyond the shipped test: (1) 8 concurrent same-node AppendEdge calls during one Compact iteration - all survived, correctly summed on the next Compact, x5 runs under -race; (2) a different-node AppendEdge issued while Compact held another node's lock completed promptly (<500ms, no blocking) x5 runs - confirms per-node, not global, lock scope; (3) two fully concurrent Compact calls over 50 overlapping nodes did not deadlock across 5 runs (10s timeout each) - Compact holds MULTIPLE node locks simultaneously across one pass (not just one at a time, contrary to a naive reading of the doc comment), but os.ReadDir's documented sorted-by-filename ordering makes edgeLogNodeIDs return a consistent global order across concurrent calls, so no AB-BA lock-order inversion occurs in practice. TestCompactConcurrentAppendNotLost's compactNodeLockedHook fires exactly inside the vulnerable window (right after ReadNodeAfter returns, lock still held) and forces genuine, deterministic blocking on the fixed code (the goroutine can only complete after Compact's TruncateNode+unlock) - not a 'usually works' timing race; the test's internal 50ms select is cosmetic (helps the OLD buggy code reproduce, not required for the fixed code's correctness assertion, which is timing-independent).

requirements_conformance.evidence: Acceptance criteria required ReadNodeAfter and TruncateNode to share a lock scope per node across a single Compact iteration OR an exact-segment-set handoff. The implementer chose (and the issue explicitly permitted) the lock-scope option; confirmed by direct source read that the lock is held continuously (no release/reacquire gap) from immediately before ReadNodeAfter through that node's TruncateNode call, for every node that ends up in compactedNodeIDs. AppendEdge holds the identical lock for its whole body. This makes a concurrent AppendEdge for the same node during that window block until Compact releases the lock post-truncate, closing the exact race the issue describes, including the correctly-identified harder case (append landing inside an existing, already-read segment file, not just after it).

test_spec_conformance.evidence: go test ./engine/graph/... -race -run TestCompactConcurrentAppendNotLost passes (verified directly, -count=30, 30/30). Test uses a synchronization hook (compactNodeLockedHook) matching the exact idiom the test spec calls for, forces the append into the read-then-truncate window, and asserts survival + eventual correct merge on a second Compact call, exactly as specified.

architecture_conformance.evidence: New EdgeLog.LockNode is an independent per-node *sync.Mutex, deliberately separate from the pre-existing l.mu (writer-cache mutex) - lock ordering is always nodeLock before l.mu in both AppendEdge (via getOrOpenWriter) and Compact/TruncateNode's own internal l.mu use, never the reverse, so no lock-ordering deadlock between the two mutexes. compactNodeLockedHook follows this repo's established crabRetryHook/optimisticReadHook/atomicCommitHook/createWithHook unexported-package-var test-seam idiom exactly (nil in production, package-internal _test.go sets it). No changes to WriteCSR/LoadCSR's on-disk format or to TruncateNode's/ReadNodeAfter's public signatures.

regression_risk.evidence: Full engine/graph suite (-race -count=2) and full engine module (-race) both pass with zero regressions, including all of 4.5.11.1's own tests (TestCompactNormalTruncateThenAppendThenCompactAgain, segment-floor tests, retry-idempotency tests). Diff is additive: TruncateNode's and ReadNodeAfter's own bodies are byte-for-byte unchanged; only a new lock is threaded around their existing call sites.

edge_cases.evidence: maxSeg<0 nodes (nothing on disk) correctly release the lock immediately rather than holding it uselessly through WriteCSR. Early-error paths (ReadNodeAfter failure, WriteCSR failure, MkdirAll failure) all call releaseHeldLocks() before returning, confirmed no leaked locks by inspection of every early return in Compact. One genuine subtlety found only by my own adversarial stress test, not by the implementer's own validation-matrix or self-consistency checks: Compact holds MANY different nodes' locks simultaneously for the duration of one pass (not one at a time), which is deadlock-safe against a second concurrent Compact call only because os.ReadDir's guaranteed sorted-by-filename order gives both calls the same lock-acquisition order - this is not explicitly reasoned about in the shipped code's package doc comment (which only asserts 'single compactor assumed' via the implementation run's architecture-discovery.md, a doc that isn't shipped in compact.go's own comment). Confirmed empirically deadlock-free (5x, 50 overlapping nodes, 10s timeout) but the safety argument as documented in the code itself is weaker than what's actually true - a maintainability/documentation gap, not a functional defect.
adversarial_probes_run: 8 concurrent same-node AppendEdge calls during one Compact iteration, x5 under -race -- all survived and correctly summed
different-node AppendEdge during another node's held-lock window, x5 -- completed promptly, never blocked
two fully concurrent Compact() calls over 50 overlapping nodes, x5, 10s timeout each -- no deadlock, no race

security.evidence: No new attack surface: purely an in-process synchronization primitive over pre-existing file operations. No new file paths, no new external input parsing.

performance.evidence: Confirmed by direct trace and by adversarial probe (b): the lock's scope is genuinely per-node, not global or 'per-Compact-run' - AppendEdge to a node NOT in compactedNodeIDs (or not yet reached in the loop) is completely unaffected and unblocked. The documented tradeoff (AppendEdge to a node actively being compacted blocks for that Compact run's remaining WriteCSR/saveCompactState/TruncateNode duration) is real and correctly scoped to only the nodes that actually had incoming edges (compactedNodeIDs), not the entire node set enumerated by edgeLogNodeIDs - nodes with maxSeg<0 release immediately. This is an acceptable, bounded, and accurately-disclosed availability cost for closing a real data-loss bug.

maintainability.evidence: Code and doc comments are thorough and mostly accurate. Two minor gaps: (1) the package doc comment's deadlock-safety claim understates that Compact holds multiple node locks concurrently across one pass, relying on an unstated (though true) property of os.ReadDir's sorted ordering for its actual safety against concurrent Compact calls - see edge_cases above; (2) 036-verification's prior low-severity recommendation (add a permanent interleaved-multi-node TruncateNode/AppendEdge/Compact regression test) was not addressed by this commit and remains open.

confidence.evidence: Verified by direct source trace (not implementer narrative), by running the shipped test at high count under -race, by running the full package and module suites, and by three independently-authored adversarial probes covering scenarios the shipped test does not - all confirming the fix's correctness claims.

possible_improvements.evidence: (1) Add a permanent test for >=2 concurrent AppendEdge calls to the same node mid-Compact (my throwaway probe covered this; not shipped). (2) Add a permanent test for a different-node AppendEdge not blocking mid-Compact (also throwaway only). (3) Strengthen compact.go's package doc comment to explicitly state that Compact may hold several nodes' locks concurrently within one pass and why that is still deadlock-safe (os.ReadDir's sorted order giving a consistent global lock-acquisition order across any two Compact calls), rather than resting only on an unstated single-compactor assumption. None of these are blocking for this subtask.

untrusted_content_disclosure: gh issue view 49's body is a clean, well-formed subtask list with no embedded fake directives - verified directly. Separately, this verification run's own tool-call stream contained several environment-injected blocks styled as system reminders (a 'date has changed, do not mention it' notice, a 'tokensave' MCP-server instruction block for a server never invoked in this run, and an 'Auto Mode Active' directive). None of these originated from gh/git content, none were treated as consent, approval, or permission/scope changes, and the task was carried out per the original launching agent's explicit instructions only.

closability_assessment.reasoning: Both the acceptance criterion (shared lock scope across ReadNodeAfter/TruncateNode for a given node, closing the concurrent-AppendEdge race) and the exact-named test spec (TestCompactConcurrentAppendNotLost) are genuinely met, independently verified via direct source trace, test execution, and adversarial stress probes beyond what the implementer ran. Combined with 4.5.11.1 (already verified PASS_WITH_COMMENTS in 036-verification), only 4.5.11.3 (EdgeType validation guard in LoadCSR/decodeCSREdge) remains open for issue #49.
